coredata.dc:title: Construction of highly active Fe/N-CQDs/MCN1 photocatalytic self-Fenton system for degradation of ciprofloxacin
coredata.dc:creator: Meng, Donghuan
Lei, Ye
Pang, Menghan
Qiu, Jianrong
Fan, Chenghua
Feng, Yuyan
Wang, Dongbo
coredata.dc:description: In the graphitic carbon nitride-based photocatalytic reaction, the redox ability and electron transport ability of photocatalyst have an important influence on its activity. The Photo-Fenton reaction system was constructed by modifying N-doped carbon quantum dots (N-CQDs) on the surface of supramolecular self-assembled carbon nitride and introducing Fe ions into the carbon nitride planar structure. The xFe/N-CQDs/MCN1 catalysts with different iron contents were prepared, which had significant degradation effect on organic pollutants. Among them, 0.2Fe/N-CQDs/MCN1 degraded 76% of ciprofloxacin (CIP) within 120 min. Various characterizations demonstrate that photocatalytic activity are enhanced due to the surface modification of N-CQDs enhancing electron transfer rate on the surface of photocatalysts and the doping of Fe ions providing active sites for Fenton reaction. At the same time, the formation of chemical bonds limits the leaching of iron and avoids secondary environmental pollution. These results indicate that the novel catalyst has excellent application potential in the field of environmental photocatalysis.

head.ce:title: Construction of highly active Fe/N-CQDs/MCN1 photocatalytic self-Fenton system for degradation of ciprofloxacin

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In the graphitic carbon nitride-based photocatalytic reaction, the redox ability and electron transport ability of photocatalyst have an important influence on its activity. The Photo-Fenton reaction system was constructed by modifying N-doped carbon quantum dots (N-CQDs) on the surface of supramolecular self-assembled carbon nitride and introducing Fe ions into the carbon nitride planar structure. The xFe/N-CQDs/MCN1 catalysts with different iron contents were prepared, which had significant degradation effect on organic pollutants. Among them, 0.2Fe/N-CQDs/MCN1 degraded 76% of ciprofloxacin (CIP) within 120 min. Various characterizations demonstrate that photocatalytic activity are enhanced due to the surface modification of N-CQDs enhancing electron transfer rate on the surface of photocatalysts and the doping of Fe ions providing active sites for Fenton reaction. At the same time, the formation of chemical bonds limits the leaching of iron and avoids secondary environmental pollution. These results indicate that the novel catalyst has excellent application potential in the field of environmental photocatalysis.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Semiconductor photocatalysis is a promising clean and renewable energy technology that can convert solar energy into chemical energy and degrade organic pollutants in the environment [1–4]. Graphite-phase carbon nitride (g-C3N4) is a representative and promising visible photocatalyst in recent years, which has been widely studied because of its unique electronic band structure and excellent photocatalytic performance [5–7]. However, g-C3N4 synthesized by traditional thermal polymerization has many weaknesses, such as fewer reactive sites, small specific surface area, easy recombination of photoelectron-hole and low carrier mobility, which lead to its low photocatalytic activity and limit application in actual water environment [8–11]. Therefore, the construction of stable and efficient g-C3N4-based visible light catalytic system is an important topic that needs to be solved both in theoretical basic research and practical application of photocatalysis, which has high research value and great scientific significance.

So far, many studies have tried to optimize the photocatalytic performance of g-C3N4 by constructing heterojunctions [12–16], constructing Photocatalysis-Fenton system, adjusting morphology [17,18], doping with metal or non-metal elements [19], and increasing defects. Taking supramolecular self-assembled g-C3N4 as an example [20], it shows larger surface area, more orderly molecular structure and better photocatalytic performance than bulk g-C3N4, and supramolecular self-assembly is a critical method to modulate morphology [21–24]. Furthermore, N-CQDs modification is an essential factor in enhancing the visible light absorption intensity, broadening the absorption range and improving electron transfer of composite photocatalysts [1,25–29].

Fenton reaction is to catalyze the formation of hydroxyl radical (·OH) with strong oxidation ability by the chain reaction between iron divalent ion (Fe2+) and hydrogen peroxide (H2O2), which can oxidize various organic compounds that are difficult to degrade [23,24,30]. Iron-based materials are generally used as catalysts in Fenton reaction, but their wide application is limited by their poor dispersity and secondary pollution caused by iron leaching [25]. g-C3N4, as an n-type photocatalyst with electron conduction as the main body, has strong electron reduction due to its negative conduction band position. Nevertheless, many g-C3N4-based photocatalysts have low H2O2 utilization rate and cannot produce ·OH for its valence band (VB) position is lower than the standard redox potential of ·OH/OH- [26,31–33]. Therefore, by coupling Fenton reaction and photocatalytic reaction to build a photofenton system, it is expected to improve the utilization rate of H2O2 and generate highly oxidizing ·OH through Fenton reaction, thus achieving efficient degradation of organic pollutants [34,35]. Many studies have proved that the incorporation of iron in different forms into g-C3N4 can improve the degradation efficiency of pollutants by constructing the photofenton reaction. For example, Wang et al. [36] have synthesized Fe ion-doped g-C3N4 (Fe/CN) porous nanosheets by using inexpensive urea and Fe(NO3)3·9 H2O as reactants. Fe/CN has higher photocatalytic activity due to its photofenton reaction mechanism, which can generate H2 and remove pollutants in wastewater. Moreover, because of the intra-ring structure of g-C3N4 with single-pair electrons of six N atoms, it is more suitable for Fe doping or complexation and prevents Fe leaching. Qin et al. [21] have prepared magnetic Fe3O4 quantum dots modified Bi2O4/g-C3N4 composite photocatalyst by combining calcination with hydrothermal reaction. Due to the synergistic effect of Z-scheme heterojunction and Fenton reaction, that photocatalyst is able to generate strong oxidizing ·OH while maintaining the original strong reducing electrons. Thus, the photocatalytic performance is greatly improved.

As we all known, organic pollutants are organic compounds that are discharged into environment and contaminate environment. Water pollution and water shortage crisis cause widely following and reporting. A large number of new-type trace antibiotics have attracted attention because of its low content in the aquatic environment, the characteristic of complex components and hardly self-degradable. Especially, CIP and tetracycline hydrochloride (TC-HCl) can cause toxic effects, such as bioaccumulation and cancer [8,37,38]. Carbon nitride based photocatalysts can produce H2O2, but their utilization rates are not high. Due to the energy band structure, they cannot form hydroxyl radical (·OH) with strong oxidizing ability [39]. In this study, Fe ions and N-CQDs were introduced into supramolecular self-assembly of carbon nitride to synthesize a novel photocatalytic self-Fenton catalyst through simple ultrasonic deposition and thermal polymerization. N-CQDs were used to improve the mobility of photocarriers, meanwhile the chain reaction between divalent Fe ion (Fe2+) and H2O2 catalyzed the formation of ·OH with strong oxidation ability, so as to oxidize various organic pollutants that are difficult to degrade. Under visible light, the photocatalytic performance of novel self-Fenton catalysts removeing organic pollutants were investigated by simulating wastewater containing various of organic pollutants, which were hardly degraded and low concentrations. Then mechanism of the photofenton catalytic reaction was clarified by means of material structure, photoelectric chemical properties, active groups and other characterization analysis methods. Therefore, this work provides inspiration for the development of other materials with excellent performance.

All materials were of analytical grade and used as received without further treatment. CIP, Bisphenol A (BA), melamine and cyanuric acid purchased from Macklin Biochemical Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). Ethylenediamine tetraacetic acid disodium (EDTA-2Na), isopropyl alcohol (IPA), absolute ethanol, dimethyl sulfoxide (DMSO) were obtained from Guanghua Sci-Tech Co., Ltd. (Guangdong, China). Citric acid, Fe(NO3)3·9 H2O and TC-HCl were supplied by Aladdin Biochemical Technology Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). Urea and p-benzoquinone (BQ) were obtained from Sinopham Chemical Reagent Corp, P. R. China.

Bulk g-C3N4 (BCN) was synthesized by thermal polymerization. 3.0 g melamine was put into a covered ceramic crucible, and then calcined in a muffle furnace at 550 ℃ for 3 h (heating rate of 5 ℃·min−1). After cooling to room temperature, the product was grounded. The obtained yellow powder was BCN.

N-CQDs were prepared by hydrothermal method with a small modification [6]. N-CQDs/MCN1 was synthesized by supramolecular self-assembly method. Briefly, 1 g of melamine and 1.02 g of cyanuric acid were dissolved in 40 mL DMSO respectively, then stirred for 1 h. 1 mL N-CQDs solution was added into cyanuric acid solution, and ultrasonicated for 30 min to mix uniformly. After mixing melamine solution with the cyanuric acid solution of N-CQDs, white suspended solid was obtained and stood for 2 h. The white suspended matter was washed several times with ethanol and deionized water, then dried to powder at 60 ℃. The white powder was transferred into a ceramic crucible with a cover, and continued heating at 550 ℃ for 3 h (heating rate of 5 ℃·min−1). After cooling to room temperature, the light yellow powder was recorded as N-CQDs/MCN1 ( 1 represents 1 mL N-CQDs solution).

Supramolecular self-assembled carbon nitride (MCN) was prepared in the same way as above method without N-CQDs. The melamine-cyanuric acid supramolecular aggregate (MCA) was directly prepared without N-CQDs and calcination.

The photofenton system was further constructed on N-CQDs/MCN1 photocatalyst. 1 g melamine and 1.02 g cyanic acid were respectively dissolved in 40 mL DMSO and stirred until dissolved. 1 mL N-CQDs solution was added into cyanic acid solution, and the mixture was homogenized by ultrasonicated for 30 min. The mixture of melamine and cyanuric acid solution with N-CQDs was stood for 2 h, washed several times with ethanol, and dried into powder at 60 ℃. The powder was dispersed in 20 mL deionized water and stirred magnetically for 30 min 0.1, 0.2 and 0.5 mL Fe(NO3)3·9 H2O solution (10 g·L−1) were added into the suspension, respectively, and stirred for another 30 min. After ultrasonic treatment for 1 h, the suspension was dried in an oven at 60 °C until the moisture was completely removed. The dried powder was transferred to a ceramic crucible and calcined at 550 ℃ for 3 h in a muffle furnace (heating rate of 5 ℃·min−1). After cooling to room temperature, the collected powder was xFe/N-CQDs/MCN (x = 0.1, 0.2, 0.5 mL Fe(NO3)3·9 H2O solution). The contents of Fe(NO3)3·9 H2O corresponding to x were 0.12, 0.24 and 0.6 μM, respectively.
Similar to the above method, 0.2Fe/MCN catalyst was obtained by adding 0.2 mL Fe(NO3)3·9 H2O solution (10 g·L−1) on the basis of preparing MCN.

The morphology were characterized by scanning electron microscope (SEM, S-3400 N). The crystalline phases of the samples were characterized on an X-ray diffraction (XRD, Rigaku, D/MAX 2500 V, Japan) with Cu Kα (λ = 1.5406 Å) irradiation at 40 kV and 100 mA. Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectra were recorded with IRTracer-100 spectrometer. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) measurements were conducted by a Thermo Scientific K-Alpha Nexsa equipped with standard monochromatic Al Kα excitation source. The optical properties of samples were investigated through a UV–vis spectrophotometer (UV–vis DRS, UV-3600Plus) and fluorescence spectrophotometer (PL, FL3C-111).

In order to evaluate the performance of the prepared photocatalysts, a 300 W xenon lamp (cut-off filter < 420 nm) was used to simulate the degradation of CIP, BA and TC-HCl under visible light. Specifically, 25 mg of photocatalyst was dispersed in 50 mL (15 mg·L−1) CIP solution and stirred in the dark for 30 min to achieve adsorption desorption equilibrium before illumination. When the light was turned on, about 4 mL of reaction suspension was extracted at a fixed interval of 30 min and filtered through a 0.22 µm membrane filter, then its concentration was measured by a UV-Vis spectrophotometer at 277 nm. In addition, the concentrations of BA and TC-HCl were both 10 mg·L−1.

The quenching experiments were employed to ascertain the role of various kinds of reactive species in the CIP photodegradation process. BQ, IPA and EDTA-2Na were added to the pollutant solution as scavengers for superoxide radical (·O2 -), hydroxyl radical (·OH) and hole (h+), respectively.

The transient photocurrent response, electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) and Mott-Schottky (M-S) plots of the samples were measured on an electrochemical workstation (CHI660E) with Ag/AgCl as the reference electrode. The working electrode was obtained by dip-coating method. A certain amount of catalyst powder was dispersed in 2 mL of anhydrous ethanol to produce suspension, which was then coated onto a FTO glass electrode. Furthermore, the electrolyte was 0.1 M Na2SO4 aqueous solution.

The XRD patterns of the samples are shown in Fig. 1a. The diffraction patterns of N-CQDs/MCN1 are similar to those of MCN. Two characteristic diffraction peaks of g-C3N4 (JCPDS No. 87–1526) appear at about 12.8° and 27.4°, corresponding to (100) and (002) crystal planes respectively [40]. The crystallinity of xFe/ N-CQDs /MCN1 series samples and 0.2Fe/MCN sample increase significantly after Fe doping, indicating that Fe doping makes the unit structure of carbon nitrite become orderly, while the peak intensities of (100) and (002) crystal planes decrease gradually with the increase of Fe doping content. It indicates that excessive Fe doping will redestroy the periodic ordering of MCN. Compared with N-CQDs/MCN1 and MCN, the (002) crystal plane of 0.2Fe/N-CQDs/MCN1 is 27.2°, indicating that its layer spacing increases and its morphology and structure are stripped [32]. There are no characteristic peaks of ferro-nitrogen compounds, ferro-oxygen compounds and ferro-carbon compounds in the XRD patterns, indicating that Fe enters into the skeleton structure of MCN in simple or ionic form. The specific chemical structure will be further proved by XPS. FT-IR of the samples are shown in Fig. 1b. The absorption peaks at 810 cm−1 and 880 cm−1 are the bending vibrations of the s-triazine unit and the triazine unit, respectively [41]. With the increase of Fe doping, the absorption peaks of s-triazine unit gradually increase, and the peaks of the triazine ring correspondingly weaken. The reason may be that Fe in the plane structure of MCN tends to be doped into s-triazine unit of MCN by forming coordination bonds with six N atoms (Fe－N), so the incorporation of Fe into MCA can make carbon nitride form a more stable s-triazine unit in the process of thermal polymerization. Simultaneously, the absorption peaks in the range of 1200–1700 cm−1 are caused by tensile vibration of several typical C－N heterocycles [42]. With the increase of Fe doping amount, the vibration peaks here have obvious changes, indicating that the formation of Fe－N coordination bond has an effect on the vibration of C－N heterocycles. The wideband peak at 3000–3600 cm−1 is caused by the stretching vibration of amine group (N－H) and hydroxyl group (O－H) [43]. The intensity of the wideband peak tends to weaken with the doping of Fe, which may be caused by the formation of Fe－N coordination bond reducing the electron density of N atoms. XRD and FT-IR analysis showe that Fe is successfully doped into the plane structure of MCN as a simple or ionic form, and Fe－N coordination bonds are formed through coordination with six N atoms in heptazine structure. In addition, appropriate Fe doping can improve the crystallinity of photocatalysts and improve their photocatalytic performance.

The samples were analyzed by SEM. SEM images of MCN and N-CQDs/MCN1 are shown in Fig. S1. As seen in Fig. 2a, 0.2Fe/MCN is a highly exfoliated tubular structure formed by stacked nanosheets in the tube, with many exfoliated crimped nanosheets scattered around the tube. As shown in Fig. 2b-d, adding Fe in N-CQDs/MCN1 has an effect on the morphology, and the microstructure of xFe/N-CQDs/MCN1 are separated from pinecone spheres into tubular loofah structure. With the increase of Fe doping amount, the materials are exfoliated more obviously, hence 0.5Fe/N-CQDs/MCN1 is completely exfoliated into broken and crimped nanosheets, indicating that appropriate Fe doping can induce the material to form the exfoliated tubular structure. It is inferred that Fe doping promotes the recombination of material structure, thus affecting its apparent morphology. EDS analysis (Fig. 2e-h) show that C, N, O and Fe are the main elements in 0.2Fe/N-CQDs/MCN1, and C, N, O and Fe are evenly distributed on the material, indicating that Fe is distributed in the framework of heptazine ring instead of forming Fe-containing compounds for binding at the nanoscale. The specific elements content are shown in Table 1, and the effective amount of Fe incorporated into MCN is 0.07 wt%.

The influence of Fe doping on the surface groups and chemical valence states of samples was further studied. 0.2Fe/N-CQDs/MCN1 and N-CQDs/MCN1 were characterized by XPS as shown in Fig. 3. According to the full spectrum of samples (Fig. 3a), 0.2Fe/N-CQDs/MCN1 and N-CQDs/MCN1 are mainly composed of C, N and O, and no other elements are detected. Due to the small amount of Fe doping, Fe element could not be detected in 0.2Fe/N-CQDs/MCN1. As shown in Fig. 3b-d, the components of each element are analyzed through high resolution spectrum. Fig. 3b is the C 1 s spectra of 0.2Fe/N-CQDs/MCN1 and N-CQDs/MCN1, and both samples show characteristic diffraction peaks at 284.8, 287.1 and 288.2 eV, corresponding to C－C, C－OH and N－C＝N bonds, respectively [44,45]. The C－OH bond is derived from surface group of N-CQDs, and the other bonds are typical characteristic peaks of carbon nitride. Fig. 3c shows the N 1 s spectra of the materials. Three characteristic peaks, 398.7 eV, 399.8 eV and 404.9 eV, are corresponding to C－N＝C bond, sp3 hybrid nitrogen (N－(C)3) linked to carbon atoms, and π-π * excited charge effect, respectively [46]. The binding energies of C－N＝C and N－(C)3 bonds in 0.2Fe/N-CQDs/MCN1 decrease by about 0.4 eV. These changes are attributed to the formation of Fe－N coordination bonds, which affect the chemical environment and electronic structure of N atoms and increase electron cloud density [47,48]. In addition, it can be observed that in the N 1 s spectrum of 0.2Fe/N-CQDs/MCN1, the area of C－N＝C peak at 398.3 eV increases. This is because the lone pair electrons in the C－N＝C bonds are more likely to capture Fe and form coordination bonds. Therefore, the doping of Fe leads to the formation of more s-triazine structures in the thermal polymerization process. This corresponds to the higher crystallinity of carbon nitride doped with Fe and the transformation of triazine structure to stable s-triazine structure in XRD and FT-IR. Fig. 3d shows Fe 2 p spectra of 0.2Fe/N-CQDs/MCN1. Peaks at 711.5 and 724.8 eV correspond to Fe 2 p3/2 and 2 p1/2 orbitals, respectively [49,50]. These two peaks reveal the presence of Fe2+ and Fe3+ ions in the material, and no peak associated with zero-valent Fe is detected. This provides a theoretical basis for the occurrence of Fenton reaction.

The optical properties of N-CQDs/MCN1, 0.2Fe/MCN and xFe/N-CQDs/MCN1 were investigated by UV–vis DRS. As shown in Fig. 4a, the absorption spectrum of N-CQDs/MCN1 presents an absorption edge with a position of about 456 nm, which is consistent with previous studies [51]. Compared with N-CQDs/MCN1, the absorption edges of xFe/N-CQDs/MCN1 appear red shift, and the light absorption of 200–700 nm region are significantly enhanced, which are due to the change of band structure position and the enhancement of light absorption caused by Fe element doping [52]. With the increase of Fe doping amount, the light absorption of xFe/N-CQDs/MCN1 gradually increase. The Eg energy of 0.2Fe/N-CQDs/MCN1 is 2.44 eV according to its Tauc diagram (Fig. 4a illustration). UV–vis DRS results show that 0.2Fe/N-CQDs/MCN1 has stronger light absorption in visible light, and the light absorption range is also widened, which are related to the influence of Fe ion doping on the band gap. The narrower the band gap, the stronger the light absorption [53,54]. In order to verify the synergy effect of N-CQDs modifying and Fe doping, PL spectra was measured to explore migration situation of electrons and holes and electron-hole recombination rates in photocatalysts. The lower electron-hole recombination rate, which proves effective migration degree of electrons and holes, the higher photocatalytic activity. As shown in Fig. 4b, the materials are excited by the excitation light source with a wavelength of 365 nm, and all have strong fluorescence emission centers near 450 nm. Compared with N-CQDs/MCN1, the fluorescence emission intensity of xFe/N-CQDs/MCN1 doped with Fe ions decrease significantly, but at the same time decrease first and then increase with the increase of Fe ion content, indicating that Fe ion doping has an inhibitory effect on electron-hole pairs recombination. This is because the formation of H2O2 and the transformation of Fe3+ to Fe2+ require the participation of electrons, moreover the large and continuous consumption of e- reduces the recombination rate with h+. The fluorescence emission intensity of 0.2Fe/MCN without N-CQDs is higher than photocatalysts with N-CQDs, indicating that the modification of N-CQDs leads to rapid e- transfer, which is the premise of the reduction of electron-hole pair recombination rate.

Photoelectrochemical research were studied to further explore the charge carrier migration behavior of the photocatalysts. The transient photocurrent response spectrum of samples are shown in Fig. 5a. Photocatalyst 0.2Fe/N-CQDs/MCN1 has the maximum charge density under light, while it can be observed that its photocurrent response gradually weakens under visible light irradiation after 5 cycles of dark-light cycle. Finally, the electron density of 0.2Fe/N-CQDs/MCN1 is slightly weaker than that of MCN and N-CQDs/MCN1. Electrons corresponding to photocurrent decrease of 0.2Fe/N-CQDs/MCN1 participate in the Fenton reaction cycle in the electrolyte rather than the photocurrent circuit, which is different from simple photocatalysis of MCN and N-CQDs/MCN1 [9,10,12]. It is deduced that the electron transfer path of 0.2Fe/N-CQDs/MCN1 is different from that of MCN and N-CQDs/MCN1. It also reacts with H+ to generate H2O2 to participate in Fenton reaction. Consequently, electron transferred to the chain of Fenton reaction is why photocurrent response of 0.2Fe/N-CQDs/MCN1 gradually decreases. As shown in Fig. 5b, the radius of the 0.2Fe/N-CQDs/MCN1 Nyquist curve in EIS is between MCN and N-CQDs/MCN1. The radius of the charge transfer resistance (Rct) curve is corresponding to the charge transfer rate of photocatalyst, suggesting that 0.2Fe/N-CQDs/MCN1 has rapider charge transfer rate than MCN [55]. The above characterization results indicate that Fe ion doping affects the photoelectron transport path, but it still maintains excellent photocharge transfer ability similar to N-CQDs/MCN1. The conduction band (CB) position of 0.2Fe/N-CQDs/MCN1 photofenton catalyst was determined by M-S plots. In Fig. 5c, the positive slope of the M-S curve identifies photocatalyst 0.2Fe/N-CQDs/MCN1 as an n-type semiconductor, similar to most carbon nitride based photocatalyst materials. The tangent line of M-S curve shows that the flat band potential (EFB) of 0.2Fe/ N-CQDs /MCN1 is − 0.91 V (νs. Ag/AgCl), corresponding to − 0.69 V (νs. NHE). For n-type semiconductors, EFB is generally considered as its conduction potential (ECB) [5,56,57]. Therefore, the valence band potential (EVB) of 0.2Fe/N-CQDs/MCN1 is calculated to be 1.75 V based on Eg and ECB. Comparing with the band structure of N-CQDs/MCN1 and MCN in Fig. S2, photofenton catalyst 0.2Fe/N-CQDs/MCN1 basically maintains the lower CB position, however its narrower band gap is similar to that of N-CQDs/MCN1. It can be predicted that 0.2Fe/N-CQDs/MCN1 photofenton catalyst can increase Fenton reaction with strong oxidation while maintaining its good photocatalytic activity to ensure excellent degradation performance.

The catalytic activity of the composites were evaluated by degrading organic pollutants in visible light. In previous studies, as shown in Fig. S3, the photocatalytic degradation performance of TC-HCl by N-CQDs/MCN series materials were explored, and the best material was N-CQDs/MCN1. With 15 mg·L−1 CIP as the target pollutant and 25 mg per catalyst, the degradation curve of CIP under visible light are shown in Fig. 6a. Without photocatalyst in the blank sample, the pollutant itself cannot be degraded under light. The xFe/N-CQDs/MCN1 series samples show stronger adsorption effect after reaching the adsorption equilibrium in the dark reaction for 1 h, because their active sites increase due to Fe doping. After turning on the xenon lamp, 0.2Fe/N-CQDs/MCN1 showes a better degradation effect within 120 min, and the degradation rate of target pollutant CIP reaches 76%. Compared with MCN (11%) and N-CQDs/MCN1 (32%), its degradation rate increases by 65% and 44%, respectively. In addition, the degradation rate of 0.2Fe/MCN for CIP is between N-CQDs/MCN1 and xFe/N-CQDs/MCN1, which further proves that N-CQDs provides Fenton reaction with electrons of stronger reduction performance and more H2O2. Therefore, the degradation performance of xFe/N-CQDs/MCN1 composites are improved by the interaction of N-CQDs and Fe ion doping. The degradation rate of xFe/N-CQDs/MCN1 increases first and then decreases with the increase of Fe ion addition content, because excessive Fe ion doping will destroy the ordered structural unit of MCN, causing the structure collapse of the triazine unit or s-triazine unit of carbon nitride. This is consistent with the results of XRD and PL. At the same time, excessive Fe doping will coalesce into larger particles and form compounds. It is not conducive to Fenton reaction. Fig. 6b shows the fitting results of reaction kinetics constants of samples. Compared with MCN and N-CQDs/MCN1, the rate constants of xFe/N-CQDs/MCN1 increase significantly. And with the increase of Fe ion doping amount, k value increases first and then decreases. The rate constant of 0.2Fe/N-CQDs/MCN1which shows the best activity (0.009 min−1), is 3.5 times higher than that of N-CQDs/MCN1 (0.002 min−1). On the one hand, the degradation efficiencies of 0.2Fe/N-CQDs/MCN1 for TC-HCl and BA have already been evaluated under visible light illumination, which are 92% and 68% within 120 min, respectively (Fig. S4). On the other hand, the photodegradation efficiencies of coexisting CIP and TC-HCl were performed by 0.2Fe/N-CQDs/MCN1 under visible light. As shown in Fig. S5, 70% of CIP (at 277 nm) and 78% of TC-HCl (at 357 nm) are removed and they are lower than the photodegradation efficiencies of individual pollutants, especially TC-HCl. The reason might be that more CIP is absorbed and photodegraded at first by 0.2Fe/N-CQDs/MCN1. Accordingly, it is indicated that 0.2Fe/N-CQDs/MCN1 can be applied to different organic pollutants removal. Compared with other researches on g-C3N4-based photocatalysts, 0.2Fe/N-CQDs/MCN1 is a photo-self-Fenton system with excellent photodegradation activity (Table S1).

According to the degradation efficiencies of 0.2Fe/N-CQDs/MCN1 photofenton catalyst for CIP under different pH and initial concentrations of CIP, the optimal reaction conditions were explored. The experimental results are shown in Fig. 7a-b. When pH is 3, the material has the best degradation effect, and the degradation rate of CIP reaches 84% within 120 min. When pH is 7, the degradation rate of CIP is 77% within 120 min. H2O2, the important intermediate product of photofenton system, is governed by the following reaction: O2 + 2 H+ + 2e- → H2O2, hence the photocatalytic degradation efficiency in solution with a pH range of 3–7 is better than that in alkaline solution [9]. It is inferred that 0.2Fe/N-CQDs/MCN1 can still maintain high activity in neutral actual water, and can be applied to water environment at a wide pH range. When the initial concentration of CIP is 10 mg·L−1, the degradation curve drops rapidly at first and then rises slightly, because the concentration of pollutant is too low, the reaction rate is too fast, and the intermediate products generated by CIP affect the determination of concentration. When the initial concentration of CIP is 15 mg·L−1, pollutant is degraded more stably than 10 mg·L−1 CIP, and its degradation rate is higher than that of 20 mg·L−1 CIP. Since the pseudo-first-order model is discussed by many authors, Langmuir–Hinshelwood model is also used to investigate the photocatalytic degradation of CIP (Fig. S6) [58–60].

The mineralization degree of 0.2Fe/N-CQDs/MCN1 and N-CQDs/MCN1 in the catalytic degradation of CIP were determined and analyzed by total organic carbon (TOC). Fig. 8a shows that after catalytic reaction for 2 h, the degradation rate of 0.2Fe/N-CQDs/MCN1 for CIP reaches 76%, and its mineralization rate is 48% which is 36% higher than that of N-CQDs/MCN1. This indicates that CIP is first converted into other intermediate products in the degradation process, and then decomposed into CO2 and H2O. Because Fenton reaction and photocatalytic reaction are carried out simultaneously, the mineralization rate of the material is faster than that of the single photocatalytic reaction. In order to further investigate the stability and recyclability of 0.2Fe/N-CQDs/MCN1, it was washed with deionized water and dried in an oven before reuse. As shown in Fig. 8b, after four cycles, the photodegradation efficiency of CIP decreases from 76% to 69%, which is a slight decrease [61]. It is demonstrated that 0.2Fe/N-CQDs/MCN1 has excellent stability and can be recycled.

In order to investigate the main reactive species in the photocatalytic process, BQ, IPA and EDTA-2Na were selected as quenchers of ·O2 -, ·OH and h+, respectively. The degradation efficiencies of CIP are shown in Fig. 9a. The addition of BQ and IPA have a great inhibitory effect on the degradation of CIP, indicating that ·O2 - and ·OH are the main active substances in the degradation process of CIP. Meanwhile, with the addition of EDTA-2Na, the degradation rate of CIP also decreases to a certain extent, indicating that h+ also plays a certain role in the degradation of CIP, but h+ is not the main active substance in comparison. Electron paramagnetic resonance (EPR) was used to identify free radicals generated by 0.2Fe/N-CQDs/MCN1, as shown in Fig. 9b-c. No relevant signal was detected in 0.2Fe/N-CQDs/MCN1 photocatalyst under dark condition, proving that ·O2 - and ·OH did not exist in water environment before illumination. With illumination for 15 min, DMPO molecules react with ·O2 - and ·OH to form DMPO-·O2 - and DMPO-·OH, and corresponding signal peaks appear in the spectrum, meanwhile the signals become stronger with the extension of illumination time. The results show that ·O2 - and ·OH are the main active species in 0.2Fe/N-CQDs/MCN1 system. As seen in Fig. S7, the EPR result of N-CQDs/MCN1 shows that the material cannot produce ·OH, furthermore, neither h+ on the VB of 0.2Fe/N-CQDs/MCN1 and N-CQDs/MCN1 is incapable to produce ·OH (·OH/OH- 1.99 V νs. NHE) [13,14]. Therefore, it is inferred that ·OH is generated by Fenton reaction of the material through Fe doping, which is further analyzed in the following mechanism [15,16,62].

According to the above analysis results, the photocatalytic self-Fenton reaction equations are put forward as shown in Eqs. (1) - (9), and the mechanism is shown in Fig. 10 [8–10,12]. When the incident light energy is greater than the material band gap energy, the e- of VB is excited to CB under visible light irradiation, and the h+ remains on VB at the same time. Subsequently, part of electrons are transferred to N-CQDs via covalent bonds C-O-C, which accelerates effective separation and transfer of photogenerated charge. The CB of 0.2Fe/N-CQDs/MCN1 is − 0.69 V (νs. NHE), which is more negative than the redox potential of O2/·O2 - (−0.33 V νs. NHE), so e- of N-CQDs reacts with O2 on the catalyst surface to form ·O2 -.·O2 - not only reacts with CIP directly, but also reacts with H+ in water to generate H2O2 (Eqs. (2)–(4)), which is consistent with transient photocurrent response, active species trapping experiments and the effect of acidity. Meanwhile, h+ of 0.2Fe/N-CQDs/MCN1 cannot oxidize OH- to ·OH because the VB position of 0.2Fe/N-CQDs/MCN1 (1.75 V) is lower than the standard redox potential of ·OH/OH- (1.99 V νs. NHE), and this is consistent with the EPR result of N-CQDs/MCN1. H2O2, as the chain propagation medium of Fenton reaction, can continuously produce ·OH by controlling the transformation between Fe3+ and Fe2+ (Eqs. (5)–(6)). Moreover, the conversion of valence states between Fe3+ and Fe2+ can be achieved by e- or ·O2 - reacting with Fe3+ (Eqs. (7)–(8)). Therefore, the amount of H2O2 generated by 0.2Fe/N-CQDs/MCN1 is the key to the continuity of Fenton reaction. The 0.2Fe/N-CQDs/MCN1 catalyst has stronger and more reductant e- due to its relatively negative CB and excellent photo-generating charge migration and separation ability, thus generating more H2O2 and allowing the Fenton reaction to proceed smoothly and spontaneously. As shown in Eq. (9), after the pollutant CIP is adsorbed on the surface of 0.2Fe/N-CQDs/MCN1, the active substances ·O2 -, ·OH and h+ produced by the catalyst can react directly with CIP. Then CIP is decomposed into intermediate products first, and finally becomes completely mineralized. Based on the above investigation and analysis, it is inferred that Fenton reaction is controlled by H2O2 of photocatalysis, but Fenton reaction has main contribution in Photo-Fenton reaction system due to its resultant ·OH. Consequently, xFe/N-CQDs/MCN1 not only keep advantages of N-CQDs/MCN1, but also possess the synergistic effect of N-CQDs and Fenton reaction on removing CIP.
(1) 0·2Fe/N-CQDs/MCN1 + hν → e- + h+
(2) e- + h+ → hν + heat energy
(3) e- + O2 →·O2 -
(4) e- +·O2 - + 2 H+ → H2O2
(5) Fe2+ + H2O2 → Fe3+ +·OH + OH-
(6) Fe3+ + H2O2 → Fe2+ + H+ +·OOH
(7) Fe3+ +·O2 - → Fe2+ + O2
(8) Fe3+ + e- → Fe2+
(9) O2 - +·OH + h+ + CIP → H2O + CO2

In summary, supramolecular self-assembled carbon nitride self-Fenton photocatalyst of Fe ion and N-CQDs, 0.2Fe/N-CQDs/MCN1 is synthesized by ultrasonic deposition and thermal polymerization. The light absorption of 0.2Fe/N-CQDs/MCN1 is enhanced efficiently and its recombination of electrons and holes are inhibited, which greatly improve its degradation performance of organic pollutants. The degradation rate of CIP by 0.2Fe/N-CQDs/MCN1 reaches 76% within 120 min in neutral water, and it has excellent degradation effect on other pollutants. The synergistic effect of Fe ion and N-CQDs is why photocatalytic activity of photocatalytic self-Fenton system is improved. On the one hand, the modification of N-CQDs can effectively separate the photoexcited electrons and holes in MCN and generate sufficient amount of e- with strong reducibility, thus affecting the generation of H2O2 and enhancing the Fenton reaction activity. On the other hand, Fe ions can assist H2O2 generated by MCN to initiate Fenton reaction. The coordination bonds formed between Fe ions and heptazine ring structure in MCN prevent Fe ions from leaching. Then Fe2+ and Fe3+ carry out valency transformation at fixed sites to generate ·OH continuously. As a conclusion, both Fenton reaction and photocatalytic reaction are able to continue stably through 0.2Fe/N-CQDs/MCN1 in wastewater under visible light illumination. Importantly, this paper provides a new idea for the development of efficient and stable composite photocatalysts.

Donghuan Meng: Investigation, Writing - review & editing. Ye Lei: Investigation, Methodology, Writing – original draft. Menghan Pang: Investigation. Jianrong Qiu: Investigation. Chenghua Fan: Investigation. Yuyan Feng: Investigation. Dongbo Wang: Conceptualization, Writing – original draft.
